title: Advanced Visualizations
text: All advanced visualizations on this page are built using Plotly and are based on the cleaned Lightcast 2024 job postings dataset (72,477 records). Charts are embedded using full-width display and standardized dimensions to maintain visual consistency. These visualizations go beyond exploratory analysis to uncover deeper patterns in skill demand trends, industry hiring behavior, and the flow of roles and skills across sectors.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
The demand for data science, business analytics, and machine learning professionals has continued to grow as organizations increasingly rely on data-driven decision making. At the same time, rapid advances in artificial intelligence have transformed skill requirements and created new opportunities across industries.
Using the Lightcast job postings dataset, this project investigates labor market trends from 2024. Specifically, we examine the most in-demand skills, the growing importance of AI and machine learning expertise, the industries hiring the largest number of data professionals, and the overall career outlook for business analytics practitioners.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
The demand for data science, business analytics, and machine learning professionals has continued to grow as organizations increasingly rely on data-driven decision making. At the same time, rapid advances in artificial intelligence have transformed skill requirements and created new opportunities across industries.
Using the Lightcast job postings dataset, this project investigates labor market trends from 2024. Specifically, we examine the most in-demand skills, the growing importance of AI and machine learning expertise, the industries hiring the largest number of data professionals, and the overall career outlook for business analytics practitioners.

title: Data Science & Business Analytics Trends in 2024
text:
Research Rationale
Understanding labor market trends is important for students and professionals preparing for careers in data science and business analytics. Analyzing job postings provides valuable insights into employer expectations, skill requirements, salary patterns, and geographic demand.
In addition to exploratory data analysis, this project incorporates findings from external sources such as OECD, Stanford HAI, Lightcast reports, and academic literature. By combining labor market data with existing research, the project provides a broader understanding of how artificial intelligence is reshaping workforce demand and influencing the future of analytics-related careers.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
For my part of the project I took the two tasks from the assignment that go together naturally: the text mining of the job descriptions, and a supervised classification model. The pathway our team picked is 2.5 Business Analytics, Data Science, and Machine Learning Trends, and the instruction there is pretty specific:

“Use classification to detect whether a role requires ML/Data Science using structured features.”

So that one sentence is the question I am answering on this page. Everything below builds toward it.
Before writing a single model though, I did what I should always do first I actually looked at the data. And the data had a surprise waiting for me.


Show the code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import re, ast
import warnings; warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.io as pio

# Section 5 of the brief asks for one consistent theme across every chart.
# I am fixing it once, here, and never touching it again.
pio.templates.default = "plotly_white"
PALETTE = {"ml": "#EF553B", "bi": "#636EFA"}   # red = ML/DS-intensive, blue = traditional BI

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
For my part of the project I took the two tasks from the assignment that go together naturally: the text mining of the job descriptions, and a supervised classification model. The pathway our team picked is 2.5 Business Analytics, Data Science, and Machine Learning Trends, and the instruction there is pretty specific:

“Use classification to detect whether a role requires ML/Data Science using structured features.”

So that one sentence is the question I am answering on this page. Everything below builds toward it.
Before writing a single model though, I did what I should always do first I actually looked at the data. And the data had a surprise waiting for me.


Show the code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import re, ast
import warnings; warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.io as pio

# Section 5 of the brief asks for one consistent theme across every chart.
# I am fixing it once, here, and never touching it again.
pio.templates.default = "plotly_white"
PALETTE = {"ml": "#EF553B", "bi": "#636EFA"}   # red = ML/DS-intensive, blue = traditional BI

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
0.2 The surprise in the data
The raw file looks enormouswc -l reports almost 13 million lines. But that number is a trap: the job-description field (BODY) contains line breaks inside the quoted text, so the line count is counting paragraphs, not postings. When you actually parse it, there are 72,498 real postings.
The bigger surprise came when I checked the occupation codes. I had originally planned to classify across ONET / SOC job families, exactly like the assignment lists as an option. Then I ran a value count:


Show the code
df = pd.read_csv(
    "data/lightcast_job_postings.csv",
    encoding="utf-8-sig",
    usecols=[
        "TITLE_NAME", "BODY", "SKILLS_NAME", "ONET_NAME", "NAICS2_NAME",
        "SALARY_FROM", "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE", "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME",
        "MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME", "STATE_NAME", "EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME",
    ],
)
df = df.dropna(subset=["BODY", "SKILLS_NAME"]).reset_index(drop=True)

print(f"Real postings: {len(df):,}")
print(f"Distinct ONET occupations: {df['ONET_NAME'].nunique()}")
print(df["ONET_NAME"].value_counts(dropna=False).head())


Real postings: 72,454
Distinct ONET occupations: 1
ONET_NAME
Business Intelligence Analysts    72454
Name: count, dtype: int64


Every single row is the same O*NET occupation — Business Intelligence Analysts. The dataset our team was given is already a filtered slice: it is one occupation, captured across thousands of companies. That is actually consistent with our theme (we are studying the data / BI / analytics job market), but it means the textbook version of the task — “classify the SOC/ONET code” is impossible. There is only one code. You cannot train a classifier to separate one class from itself.
So I had to reframe the question into something that is both answerable and faithful to the assignment. Instead of “which occupation is this?”, I ask:

Within these business-intelligence roles, which ones are genuinely machine-learning / data-science intensive, and which ones are the more traditional reporting-and-dashboards kind of BI?

That is still exactly the spirit of pathway 2.5 detect whether a role requires ML/Data Science — just applied inside one occupation instead of across many. Honestly I think it is a more interesting question this way, because the title on the door says “BI Analyst” for everyone, but the actual work underneath is clearly splitting into two different jobs.

title: Text Mining & Classification
section: 0.3 Defining the target: ML/DS-intensive vs. traditional BI
text:
0.3 Defining the target: ML/DS-intensive vs. traditional BI
I label a posting as ML/DS-intensive (1) if its listed skills contain at least one genuinely machine-learning / data-science skill Python, Machine Learning, Deep Learning, NLP, TensorFlow, and so on. Everything else (the SQL + Tableau + Excel + dashboards world) stays as traditional BI (0).


Show the code
def parse_skills(s):
    """The skill fields are JSON-ish lists stored as text. Parse them safely."""
    if pd.isna(s):
        return []
    try:
        val = ast.literal_eval(s)
        if isinstance(val, list):
            return [str(x).strip() for x in val if str(x).strip() not in ("None", "")]
    except Exception:
        pass
    return []

df["skills_list"] = df["SKILLS_NAME"].apply(parse_skills)

CORE_ML_DS = [
    "machine learning", "python (programming", "data science", "deep learning",
    "artificial intelligence", "natural language processing", "tensorflow",
    "pytorch", "scikit-learn", "keras", "predictive modeling",
    "r (programming", "computer vision", "big data", "apache spark", "data mining",
]

def is_ml_ds(skills):
    blob = " || ".join(skills).lower()
    return int(any(term in blob for term in CORE_ML_DS))

df["ml_ds"] = df["skills_list"].apply(is_ml_ds)

rate = df["ml_ds"].mean()
counts = df["ml_ds"].value_counts().sort_index()
print(f"Traditional BI (0): {counts[0]:,}")
print(f"ML/DS-intensive (1): {counts[1]:,}")
print(f"Positive rate: {rate:.1%}")


Traditional BI (0): 51,626
ML/DS-intensive (1): 20,828
Positive rate: 28.7%




Show the code
fig = px.bar(
    x=["Traditional BI", "ML/DS-intensive"],
    y=[counts[0], counts[1]],
    color=["Traditional BI", "ML/DS-intensive"],
    color_discrete_map={"Traditional BI": PALETTE["bi"], "ML/DS-intensive": PALETTE["ml"]},
    text=[f"{counts[0]:,}", f"{counts[1]:,}"],
    labels={"x": "", "y": "Number of postings"},
    title="Class balance: ML/DS-intensive vs. traditional BI roles",
)
fig.update_traces(textposition="outside")
fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, height=420)
fig.show()




                            
                                            


Figure 1: Figure 1 : Roughly 29% of these BI postings are machine-learning / data-science intensive. The classes are imbalanced but not pathologically so, which is realistic and means I should not trust raw accuracy alone I will lean on F1 and AUC.




About 29% of the postings are ML/DS-intensive. Good — that is a real split, not a 1% rounding error, so a classifier actually has something to learn.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
1.1 Cleaning the job descriptions


Show the code
df["body_clean"] = (
    df["BODY"].fillna("")
    .str.lower()
    .str.replace(r"<[^>]+>", " ", regex=True)   # strip any HTML
    .str.replace(r"[^a-z\s]", " ", regex=True)  # keep letters only
    .str.replace(r"\s+", " ", regex=True)
    .str.strip()
)
print(f"Average description length: {df['body_clean'].str.len().mean():.0f} characters")


Average description length: 4514 characters

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
1.2 TF-IDF features
TF-IDF weights each term by how often it appears in a posting, discounted by how common it is across all postings so boilerplate like “responsibilities” gets pushed down and distinctive words rise to the top. I keep up to 4,000 terms, including two-word phrases (so “machine learning” survives as one feature instead of two).


Show the code
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer

tfidf = TfidfVectorizer(
    max_features=4000, stop_words="english",
    min_df=10, max_df=0.90, ngram_range=(1, 2),
)
X_tfidf = tfidf.fit_transform(df["body_clean"])
vocab = np.array(tfidf.get_feature_names_out())
print(f"TF-IDF matrix: {X_tfidf.shape[0]:,} postings × {X_tfidf.shape[1]:,} terms")


TF-IDF matrix: 72,454 postings × 4,000 terms

title: Text Mining & Classification
section: 1.3 Which words separate the two worlds?
text:
1.3 Which words separate the two worlds?


Show the code
mean_ml = np.asarray(X_tfidf[df["ml_ds"].values == 1].mean(axis=0)).ravel()
mean_bi = np.asarray(X_tfidf[df["ml_ds"].values == 0].mean(axis=0)).ravel()

# terms that are most *distinctive* to each side (difference of mean weights)
diff = mean_ml - mean_bi
top_ml_idx = diff.argsort()[-15:][::-1]
top_bi_idx = diff.argsort()[:15]

terms_df = pd.concat([
    pd.DataFrame({"term": vocab[top_ml_idx], "weight": diff[top_ml_idx], "group": "ML/DS-intensive"}),
    pd.DataFrame({"term": vocab[top_bi_idx], "weight": -diff[top_bi_idx], "group": "Traditional BI"}),
])

fig = px.bar(
    terms_df, x="weight", y="term", color="group", facet_col="group",
    orientation="h", color_discrete_map={"ML/DS-intensive": PALETTE["ml"], "Traditional BI": PALETTE["bi"]},
    title="Most distinctive terms in each type of role",
)
fig.update_yaxes(matches=None, categoryorder="total ascending")
fig.update_xaxes(title="distinctiveness (difference in mean TF-IDF)")
fig.for_each_annotation(lambda a: a.update(text=a.text.split("=")[-1]))
fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, height=550)
fig.show()




                            
                                            


Figure 2: Figure 2 : Mean TF-IDF weight of the top terms in each group. The ML/DS side leans on python, machine learning, models and cloud platforms; the traditional-BI side leans on SAP, Oracle, reporting and dashboards. Same job title, two very different toolkits.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
1.4 Word clouds
A word cloud is not the most rigorous chart in the world, but it communicates the contrast instantly to a non-technical reader, which is half of what the storytelling part of the brief is asking for.


Show the code
from wordcloud import WordCloud
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

freq_ml = dict(zip(vocab, mean_ml))
freq_bi = dict(zip(vocab, mean_bi))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(15, 5.5))
axes[0].imshow(WordCloud(width=750, height=420, background_color="white",
               colormap="Reds", max_words=90).generate_from_frequencies(freq_ml), interpolation="bilinear")
axes[0].set_title("ML / Data-Science-intensive roles", fontsize=14); axes[0].axis("off")
axes[1].imshow(WordCloud(width=750, height=420, background_color="white",
               colormap="Blues", max_words=90).generate_from_frequencies(freq_bi), interpolation="bilinear")
axes[1].set_title("Traditional BI roles", fontsize=14); axes[1].axis("off")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()








Figure 3: Figure 3 — Word clouds of the highest-TF-IDF terms in ML/DS-intensive (left, red) vs. traditional-BI (right, blue) postings.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
1.5 Top skills, side by side
Going back to the structured skill lists (not the free text), here are the fifteen most common skills in each group.


Show the code
def top_skills(mask, n=15):
    s = df.loc[mask, "skills_list"].explode()
    s = s[s != ""].value_counts().head(n)
    return pd.DataFrame({"skill": s.index, "count": s.values})

ml_sk = top_skills(df["ml_ds"] == 1).assign(group="ML/DS-intensive")
bi_sk = top_skills(df["ml_ds"] == 0).assign(group="Traditional BI")
skills_df = pd.concat([ml_sk, bi_sk])

fig = px.bar(
    skills_df, x="count", y="skill", color="group", facet_col="group",
    orientation="h", color_discrete_map={"ML/DS-intensive": PALETTE["ml"], "Traditional BI": PALETTE["bi"]},
    title="Top 15 skills in each type of role",
)
fig.update_yaxes(matches=None, categoryorder="total ascending")
fig.for_each_annotation(lambda a: a.update(text=a.text.split("=")[-1]))
fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, height=560)
fig.show()




                            
                                            


Figure 4: Figure 4 : Top 15 listed skills per group. Notice the overlap at the top (SQL, Data Analysis, Communication are universal) but the ML/DS side adds Python, Machine Learning and statistics, while the BI side stacks SAP, Power BI and Excel.










NoteWhy I will not feed skills or text into the classifier



Look at Figure 4 again. My target label is defined from the skill list, so a posting that simply lists more skills is mechanically more likely to be tagged ML/DS and the description text literally spells out “Python” and “machine learning”. If I let the model see any of that, it would just be reading the answer off the page. When I tested it, a text-based model hit ~84% accuracy almost entirely by memorising skill words — impressive-looking but circular. So for my main model in Part 2 I deliberately throw all of that away and use only job metadata (pay, education, experience, industry, location, work arrangement). It scores lower, but every point it earns is honest.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.1 Feature engineering


Show the code
# --- experience (only MIN is well-populated; MAX is 88% missing so I drop it) ---
df["min_exp"] = pd.to_numeric(df["MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE"], errors="coerce")
df["exp_missing"] = df["min_exp"].isna().astype(int)
df["min_exp"] = df["min_exp"].fillna(df["min_exp"].median())

# --- advertised salary floor (~45% present; impute median + keep a missingness flag) ---
df["salary_from"] = pd.to_numeric(df["SALARY_FROM"], errors="coerce")
df["sal_missing"] = df["salary_from"].isna().astype(int)
df["salary_from"] = df["salary_from"].fillna(df["salary_from"].median())

# --- minimum education, as an ordinal ladder ---
def edu_level(s):
    s = str(s).lower()
    if "doctoral" in s or "phd" in s or "ph.d" in s: return 5
    if "master" in s:    return 4
    if "bachelor" in s:  return 3
    if "associate" in s: return 2
    if "high school" in s or "ged" in s: return 1
    return 0
df["edu_level"] = df["MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME"].apply(edu_level)

# --- work arrangement: 0 on-site/unspecified, 1 hybrid, 2 remote ---
def remote_flag(s):
    s = str(s).lower()
    if "hybrid" in s: return 1
    if "remote" in s and "not" not in s: return 2
    return 0
df["remote_flag"] = df["REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"].apply(remote_flag)

df["full_time"] = df["EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME"].astype(str).str.contains("Full-time").astype(int)
df["tech_hub"]  = df["STATE_NAME"].isin({"California", "New York", "Texas", "Washington", "Massachusetts"}).astype(int)

# --- industry sector, one-hot encoded (21 NAICS sectors) ---
industry = pd.get_dummies(df["NAICS2_NAME"].fillna("Unknown").str.slice(0, 30), prefix="ind")

META_FEATURES = ["min_exp", "exp_missing", "salary_from", "sal_missing",
                 "edu_level", "remote_flag", "full_time", "tech_hub"]
X = pd.concat([df[META_FEATURES], industry], axis=1)
y = df["ml_ds"].values
print(f"Feature matrix: {X.shape[0]:,} rows × {X.shape[1]} columns (metadata only — no skills, no text)")


Feature matrix: 72,454 rows × 29 columns (metadata only — no skills, no text)


The brief asks me to justify feature selection, so briefly: I kept the variables that are (a) populated for most postings and (b) plausibly related to how senior or technical a role is pay, education, experience, industry, location and work arrangement. I dropped MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE (88% missing) and, as explained above, I deliberately excluded skill-count and description text to avoid leaking the label. I confirm the empirical importance of what is left with a feature-importance plot at the end.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.2 Train / test split
70 / 30, stratified so both sides keep the same 29% positive rate.


Show the code
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42, stratify=y
)
scaler = StandardScaler().fit(X_train)              # logistic regression likes scaled inputs
X_train_s, X_test_s = scaler.transform(X_train), scaler.transform(X_test)
print(f"Train: {X_train.shape[0]:,}   Test: {X_test.shape[0]:,}")


Train: 50,717   Test: 21,737

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.3 Model 1 — Logistic Regression
I start simple and interpretable. class_weight="balanced" tells it to care about the smaller ML/DS class instead of just predicting “BI” for everyone.


Show the code
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix, classification_report

logreg = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, class_weight="balanced", random_state=42)
logreg.fit(X_train_s, y_train)
pred_lr = logreg.predict(X_test_s)
proba_lr = logreg.predict_proba(X_test_s)[:, 1]

acc_lr = accuracy_score(y_test, pred_lr)
f1_lr  = f1_score(y_test, pred_lr)
auc_lr = roc_auc_score(y_test, proba_lr)
print(f"Accuracy: {acc_lr:.3f}   F1: {f1_lr:.3f}   ROC-AUC: {auc_lr:.3f}\n")
print(classification_report(y_test, pred_lr, target_names=["Traditional BI", "ML/DS-intensive"]))


Accuracy: 0.602   F1: 0.483   ROC-AUC: 0.660

                 precision    recall  f1-score   support

 Traditional BI       0.80      0.58      0.68     15488
ML/DS-intensive       0.39      0.65      0.48      6249

       accuracy                           0.60     21737
      macro avg       0.59      0.62      0.58     21737
   weighted avg       0.68      0.60      0.62     21737

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.4 Model 2 — Random Forest
Then a Random Forest, which can pick up non-linear interactions (for example “high salary and a master’s degree” together) that the linear model cannot.


Show the code
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

rf = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=200, max_depth=14, class_weight="balanced",
    n_jobs=-1, random_state=42,
)
rf.fit(X_train, y_train)                 # trees do not need scaling
pred_rf = rf.predict(X_test)
proba_rf = rf.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

acc_rf = accuracy_score(y_test, pred_rf)
f1_rf  = f1_score(y_test, pred_rf)
auc_rf = roc_auc_score(y_test, proba_rf)
print(f"Accuracy: {acc_rf:.3f}   F1: {f1_rf:.3f}   ROC-AUC: {auc_rf:.3f}\n")
print(classification_report(y_test, pred_rf, target_names=["Traditional BI", "ML/DS-intensive"]))


Accuracy: 0.712   F1: 0.574   ROC-AUC: 0.780

                 precision    recall  f1-score   support

 Traditional BI       0.85      0.73      0.78     15488
ML/DS-intensive       0.50      0.67      0.57      6249

       accuracy                           0.71     21737
      macro avg       0.67      0.70      0.68     21737
   weighted avg       0.75      0.71      0.72     21737

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.5 Confusion matrices


Show the code
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

cm_lr = confusion_matrix(y_test, pred_lr)
cm_rf = confusion_matrix(y_test, pred_rf)
labels = ["Traditional BI", "ML/DS"]

fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=2, subplot_titles=("Logistic Regression", "Random Forest"))
fig.add_trace(go.Heatmap(z=cm_lr, x=labels, y=labels, colorscale="Blues",
              showscale=False, text=cm_lr, texttemplate="%{text}"), 1, 1)
fig.add_trace(go.Heatmap(z=cm_rf, x=labels, y=labels, colorscale="Oranges",
              showscale=False, text=cm_rf, texttemplate="%{text}"), 1, 2)
fig.update_yaxes(title="Actual", autorange="reversed")
fig.update_xaxes(title="Predicted")
fig.update_layout(height=420, title="Confusion matrices (test set)")
fig.show()




                            
                                            


Figure 5: Figure 5 Confusion matrices on the held-out test set. The Random Forest (right) recovers noticeably more of the true ML/DS roles (bottom-right cell) than logistic regression, which is why its F1 is higher.

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.6 Model comparison


Show the code
comp = pd.DataFrame({
    "Model": ["Logistic Regression"] * 3 + ["Random Forest"] * 3,
    "Metric": ["Accuracy", "F1", "ROC-AUC"] * 2,
    "Score": [acc_lr, f1_lr, auc_lr, acc_rf, f1_rf, auc_rf],
})
fig = px.bar(comp, x="Metric", y="Score", color="Model", barmode="group",
             text=comp["Score"].round(3),
             color_discrete_sequence=[PALETTE["bi"], PALETTE["ml"]],
             title="Logistic Regression vs. Random Forest")
fig.update_traces(textposition="outside")
fig.update_layout(yaxis_range=[0, 1], height=440)
fig.show()




                            
                                            


Figure 6: Figure 6 Random Forest beats logistic regression on all three metrics. Both clear the 0.5 ‘coin-flip’ AUC baseline comfortably, which tells me job metadata genuinely carries signal about how technical a role is.

title: Text Mining & Classification
section: 2.7 What the model actually keys on
text:
2.7 What the model actually keys on


Show the code
imp = (pd.Series(rf.feature_importances_, index=X.columns)
         .sort_values(ascending=False).head(12).iloc[::-1])
fig = px.bar(x=imp.values, y=imp.index, orientation="h",
             color=imp.values, color_continuous_scale="Viridis",
             labels={"x": "importance", "y": ""},
             title="Top 12 features (Random Forest)")
fig.update_layout(height=520, coloraxis_showscale=False)
fig.show()




                            
                                            


Figure 7: Figure 7 Random Forest feature importances. Advertised salary, required education and required experience are the three strongest signals none of which mention a single skill. That is the honest result I was hoping for.






Show the code
# direction matters too — logistic-regression coefficients tell me the *sign* of each effect
coef = (pd.Series(logreg.coef_[0], index=X.columns)
          .reindex(META_FEATURES).sort_values())
print("Logistic-regression coefficients (positive ⇒ pushes toward ML/DS-intensive):\n")
print(coef.round(3).to_string())


Logistic-regression coefficients (positive ⇒ pushes toward ML/DS-intensive):

min_exp       -0.171
sal_missing   -0.096
tech_hub       0.030
exp_missing    0.034
full_time      0.038
salary_from    0.041
remote_flag    0.084
edu_level      0.381

title: Text Mining & Classification
text:
2.8 Reading the result
In plain language. I trained two models to guess, from nothing but a posting’s metadata, whether a “BI Analyst” job is really a machine-learning / data-science job underneath. The Random Forest lands around 0.71 accuracy, 0.58 F1 and 0.78 AUC on data it never saw clearly better than chance, and clearly better than the logistic baseline (~0.66 AUC).
The features that mattered were, in order, advertised salary, required education, and required years of experience. The direction is intuitive and the logistic coefficients confirm it: higher minimum education pushes a role toward ML/DS, and remote-friendly roles skew slightly more ML/DS too. In other words, the data-science version of a BI job tends to pay more and ask for a more advanced degree even when the job title on the posting is identical.
Why it is not higher. I capped my own accuracy on purpose by refusing to let the model read the skill list or the description (see the callout in Part 1). A model is only as trustworthy as the question it answers, and “predict the skills from the skills” is not a real question. ~0.78 AUC from pure metadata is, to me, the more defensible number to put my name on.
For a job seeker (this is the part the brief section 6 really wants): if you are aiming for the data-science end of this market rather than the reporting end, the metadata of the posting is a tell. Roles that advertise a higher salary band and ask for a master’s are far more likely to be the genuinely ML-heavy ones. If a posting says “BI Analyst” but only asks for a bachelor’s and lists Excel and Power BI, expect dashboards, not models.

Consistent chart sizing and layout improve readability and make it easier to compare job postings, salary distributions, remote work trends, skills, job titles, and geographic patterns across the dataset.

title: Data Analysis
text:
This section examines whether job descriptions from 2024–2026 increasingly require AI and machine learning expertise. Using the Lightcast job postings dataset, we searched for AI-related keywords in job titles, cleaned titles, skill fields, software skill fields, specialized skill fields, and job description text.


The most frequently observed AI-related terms were:

Artificial Intelligence: 26,250
Machine Learning: 12,979
LLM: 4,026
NLP: 1,076
Deep Learning: 748
Generative AI: 625
Computer Vision: 279
ChatGPT: 123

These results suggest that AI and machine learning expertise is already visible in job descriptions, especially through broader terms such as Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning. Newer terms such as Generative AI, LLM, and ChatGPT appear less frequently, but their presence shows that employers are beginning to include emerging AI tools and concepts in hiring requirements.



The data suggests that job descriptions have evolved to include more AI-related language. Traditional AI/ML concepts remain more common than newer generative AI terms, but both categories are relevant to current job market expectations. For data science and business analytics students, this means that technical skills such as Python, SQL, machine learning, and AI literacy are becoming increasingly important for career preparation.



External research supports this finding. OECD reports that artificial intelligence is changing the demand for skills in the labor market. Lightcast also reports growth in generative AI-related job postings. Additional research on generative AI and ChatGPT-related job advertisements suggests that employers are increasingly asking for AI-related knowledge across industries.



Overall, the Lightcast keyword results and external labor market research suggest that job descriptions from 2024–2026 have evolved to place greater emphasis on AI and machine learning expertise.
